title: Falafel
subtitle: Crisp deep-fried chickpea fritters with herbs and spices
excerpt: Dried chickpeas soaked but not cooked, ground with onion, herbs, cumin, and coriander, then formed into patties and deep-fried until the outside is crunchy and deeply green inside. The key is using dried (not tinned) chickpeas, tinned chickpeas produce a soft, dense fritter that falls apart in the oil.
sourceNotes: Falafel is one of the signature street foods of Egypt (where it is made with broad beans and called ta'amiya), Lebanon, Israel, and the broader Levantine world. The dried-chickpea version is the Levantine standard. Claudia Roden's A Book of Middle Eastern Food (1968) documents both the Egyptian and Levantine versions. The dish became widely known in the West through Lebanese and Israeli diaspora restaurants from the 1970s onward and is now one of the most widely available street foods in cities with a significant Middle Eastern community.

content.text: The rule with falafel is: dried chickpeas only, never tinned. Tinned chickpeas are already cooked and their starches are soft and wet; when ground, they produce a paste that cannot hold together in hot oil. Dried chickpeas soaked overnight are still raw but hydrated, their starches are intact and they process into a coarse, grainy mixture that binds and fries properly. The falafel should be mixed with coriander seeds and cumin and a large amount of fresh herb, which is what gives the interior its characteristic bright green colour.

Oil temperature is critical. Too cool and the falafel absorbs oil and goes greasy; too hot and the outside burns before the inside cooks through. 180°C is the target. A thermometer helps. Without one, test with a small piece of mixture: it should sink briefly, then rise and begin fizzing actively around its edges within 5 seconds.

items.name: Dried chickpeas
items.prepNote: soaked overnight in cold water, drained

content.text: Drain the soaked {{chickpeas-dried}} thoroughly. Place in a food processor with the {{onion}}, {{garlic}}, {{parsley-flat}}, {{coriander-seeds}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{cayenne}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Pulse until the mixture is finely ground but still has some texture, not a smooth paste. It should hold together when pressed between your fingers. If it is too loose, add a tablespoon of plain flour. Refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Shape the mixture into small patties or balls about 4 cm across (a falafel scoop or a wet tablespoon helps). Roll in {{sesame-seeds}} if using. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a deep saucepan to 180°C. Fry the falafel in batches of 4 to 5 for 3 to 4 minutes, turning once, until deep golden brown all over. Drain on kitchen paper. Serve immediately with pitta, tahini sauce, and salad.

items.fix: Only dried chickpeas work here. If the mixture is too loose, add a tablespoon of flour and refrigerate for a further 30 minutes before frying. Handle as little as possible when shaping
items.cause: Mixture too wet or tinned chickpeas used instead of dried
items.symptom: Falafel falls apart in the oil

items.fix: Pulse the mixture rather than running the processor continuously, coarser texture fries better. Check doneness at 3 minutes: the outside should be dark golden but not black
items.symptom: Falafel is dense and doughy inside

items.fix: Allow the oil to reach 180°C before frying. If you don't have a thermometer, test with a small piece of mixture: it should sizzle immediately and rise to the surface within a few seconds
items.symptom: Oil is absorbed and falafel is greasy

items.fix: Use a generous amount of fresh flat-leaf parsley (some recipes also include fresh coriander). The green colour is a visual indicator of freshness and herb content

content.text: Falafel is eaten daily across Egypt, Lebanon, Syria, Palestine, Jordan, and Israel. The Egyptian version uses broad beans rather than chickpeas and is the older form; the chickpea version is the Levantine standard. In Israel, falafel in pitta is ubiquitous enough to function as a national symbol.
